reasoning_messages.reasoning:
We need to generate plan, call review_plan, etc. So respond in first message: PLAN section. That includes our plan. Steps: use run_cmd to detect if auditd uses augenrules vs auditctl, but easier: just add rule to both? But rules: we need to add to /etc/audit/rules.d/'something.rules'. Use default file name: /etc/audit/rules.d/usergroup_modification.rules.

We should read the file to check if rule exists. Use read_file. Then if not exists, we can write file with content. If exists, check if rule matches. Use sed. But simpler: create or overwrite file with correct rule line. That ensures rule is present. Use -i? Since writing new file, we can use write_file.

We must also handle audit.rules file if auditctl used. But easier: create file /etc/audit/audit.rules? But system uses augenrules? Possibly system uses augenrules. But we can add rule to both.

Thus plan: 1) Write /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_usergroup_modification.rules with line "-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification". 2) Also add to /etc/audit/audit.rules if it exists.

But need to check if file /etc/audit/audit.rules exists. Use read_file. If empty or not present, maybe ignore.

Thus plan steps:

- Determine if /etc/audit/audit.rules exists using read_file. If exists, check content. If line exists? If not, append via write_file (but can't append twice?). Use sed to insert? Could use echo >> but write_file may replace entire file. We can read content, append line to content string, then write.

So we need a plan for executing at most two commands: use individual run_cmd. Use read_file first.

Because the instructions say we need one command at a time. Implementation: read_file, then if content missing add.

Thus proposed plan:

1. Use read_file on /etc/audit/rules.d/audit_usergroup_modification.rules. If file missing, create with content.

If file present, read content; if contains rule line, do nothing; else, replace/add? Might need to insert the rule. Use sed -i to modify if absent? But we can rewrite with sed i? Actually easier: just write new file with rule line; if existing file has more lines, we might lose them; but rule likely not present; but we might override custom rules? Better to add rule line to file if not present. But given constraints, we could rewrite file with rule line only, that ensures rule present. If that file is used, no problem.

But I'm not sure which file naming. Usually system uses custom rule files with suffix .rules. Many existing ones, like 'usergroup_mod.rules'. We should pick a name: /etc/audit/rules.d/00-usergroup_modification.rules? But not specified. The remediation script states to "add the following lines to a file with suffix .rules in the directory /etc/audit/rules.d". So we need a file with same name rule? Could use /etc/audit/rules.d/user_group_mod.rules. As long as file exists, auditd will load it.

Thus plan: write file /etc/audit/rules.d/usergroup_modification.rules with content "-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification". Use write_file.

Also handle /etc/audit/audit.rules: read file; if not exist, maybe create? But it's a master file; some systems may not use it. We can read to check existence. If file exists and does not contain rule line, we need to append. To append, we could read its content, add line at end, then write back. But write_file will replace entire file. That's okay if we read content into variable, append, then write.

Thus plan:

- read_file /etc/audit/audit.rules; if return content empty? If file doesn't exist, skip.

- If content not empty and does not contain the rule line, then modify. Use pattern. Possibly use sed to delete existing rule to avoid duplicates. Or simply rewrite.

Implementation steps:

0. Plan description: set up variable for rule line.

1. Write /etc/audit/rules.d/usergroup_mod.rules with rule line.

2. Check /etc/audit/audit.rules; if exists and doesn't contain line, append line.

We'll create maybe as content: "-w /etc/passwd -p wa -k audit_rules_usergroup_modification".

Also ensure correct line endings etc.

Thus proposal seems safe.

We also need to restart auditd? Usually new rules apply automatically? Not necessary; but maybe reload daemon. But not requested.

Thus present plan to review.
